title: Closing keynote
speakers: Patrick Flaherty (Senior Motion Designer, Microsoft), Tommy Geoco (Storyteller, UX Tools   ), Catt Small (Staff Product Designer, Dropbox), Matt D. Smith (CEO, Shift Nudge), Rasmus Andersson (Software Painter, Playbit)
key_points: Patrick Flaherty's core reframe: 'Design the feel, not the graph.' The cubic Bezier curve is just a description of motion, not the goal; spacing equals velocity, and velocity equals feel, so you read a curve by watching how echo/trail spacing stacks up (slow) or opens up (fast).

The animator's timeless workflow is observe first, interpret second, stylize third, 'reference gives you the truth, taste decides what to keep, design decides how far to push.' His mantra: 'You are the analog', capture real motion (with your body, a camera, or a phone/watch accelerometer) before touching a graph editor.

Flaherty demoed a tool called Motion Tracker that records real-world motion live, tracks it on X/Y axes, and converts the timing into reusable easing curves applied directly in Figma (and After Effects). The full loop: capture, read the timing, shape the curve, apply, then name and share it as a team's shared motion language, he even wired a 'drop' curve into a Figma variable to apply it across many layers at once.

Panel data point: Figma reports 72% of attendees have tried generative AI this year; the UX Tools survey splits designers into roughly thirds, a third haven't touched AI, a third dabble, a third are fundamentally changing how they work.

Panel consensus on AI: fundamentals matter MORE because AI is a 'multiplying force' (Matt D. Smith) amplifying every good and bad decision. Designers want to own early/visioning thinking by hand and hand off production-level work to AI (Catt Small, Rasmus Andersson). Rasmus's spicy take, production design like error states and sign-up forms can be automated, but defining that you need a form is ideation. Catt: 'the double diamond is not dead.'

quotes.text: Design the feel, not the graph. The graph, it matters, of course it does, but the graph is not the goal.
